# Proof of Lemma 6.5: Sum of Solvable Ideals is Solvable

**Lemma 6.5.** If the ideals $\mathfrak{a}$ and $\mathfrak{b}$ of $\mathfrak{g}$ are solvable, then the ideal $\mathfrak{a} + \mathfrak{b}$ generated by $\mathfrak{a}$ and $\mathfrak{b}$ is solvable.

**Proof.** Consider the exact sequence of Lie algebras

$$0 \to \mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b} \to \mathfrak{a} + \mathfrak{b} \to (\mathfrak{a} + \mathfrak{b})/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b}) \to 0.$$

By the second isomorphism theorem for Lie algebras,

$$(\mathfrak{a} + \mathfrak{b})/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b}) \cong \mathfrak{a}/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b}) \oplus \mathfrak{b}/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b})$$

where the right-hand side is a direct sum of Lie algebras (the bracket between elements from the two summands vanishes modulo $\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b}$). Since $\mathfrak{a}$ is solvable, $\mathfrak{a}/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b})$ is solvable (quotient of a solvable algebra). Similarly $\mathfrak{b}/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b})$ is solvable. A direct sum of solvable Lie algebras is solvable.

Also, $\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b} \subset \mathfrak{a}$ is solvable (as a subalgebra of a solvable algebra). By Lemma 6.4, since both $\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b}$ and $(\mathfrak{a} + \mathfrak{b})/(\mathfrak{a} \cap \mathfrak{b})$ are solvable, the extension $\mathfrak{a} + \mathfrak{b}$ is solvable. $\square$

**Remark.** An immediate consequence is that every finite-dimensional Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$ possesses a unique maximal solvable ideal, called the **radical** of $\mathfrak{g}$, denoted $\mathfrak{r}$. Indeed, take $\mathfrak{r}$ to be the sum (equivalently, the ideal generated by the union) of all solvable ideals of $\mathfrak{g}$. By Lemma 6.5, $\mathfrak{r}$ is solvable, and it is clearly maximal.
